ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Danish pastry; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Danish pastry weighs approximately 80 g, equivalent to a single individually portioned piece as commonly sold in bakeries.

nutrient_source.title: Danish pastry — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Danish pastry (cr_id F11110011). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 59 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for croissant/Danish-type laminated pastry (closest analogous entry). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 59 × 43.8 / 100 = 25.8 (based on 80 g serving providing 43.8 g carbohydrate). This is classified as medium GI (56–69 range).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (25.2 g/100 g) and elevated sodium (268 mg/100 g) with high saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Danish pastry consumption, as its high sugar content drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and its saturated fat load contributes to arterial stiffness, both of which elevate blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 25.2 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; high refined carbohydrate (54.8 g/100 g) with low fibre (2.2 g/100 g) produces rapid postprandial glucose spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Danish pastry is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high sugar and refined carbohydrate content relative to its low fibre, resulting in a medium-to-high glycaemic load per serving (GL ~25.8) that causes significant postprandial glucose elevation; if consumed at all, it should be paired with protein and fibre-rich foods and portion strictly limited.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~12.5 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.3 g/100 g raise LDL cholesterol; high sugar and refined carbohydrate promote hypertriglyceridaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Danish pastry poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its combination of saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g), which raise LDL cholesterol and promote endothelial dysfunction, while its high refined sugar content contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia; regular consumption should be avoided by individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g) are strongly pro-inflammatory; refined sugar 25.2 g/100 g amplifies systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Danish pastry is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as its trans fat content is a hard pro-inflammatory trigger regardless of quantity, and its high saturated fat and refined sugar further promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity that exacerbates joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Danish pastry is a multi-layered, buttery laminated sweet pastry originating from Denmark and widely popular across Scandinavia and internationally. It is made by folding butter repeatedly into a yeast-leavened dough through a process called lamination, creating dozens of thin, flaky layers that puff and separate during baking. The result is a rich, tender pastry with a crisp exterior, soft interior, and a distinctly buttery flavour. Danish pastries are typically sweetened and may be filled with fruit jam, custard, almond paste, or cream cheese, and are often glazed with icing or sugar syrup. Per 100 g, Danish pastry provides 474 kcal, 11.5 g protein, 23.8 g fat (including approximately 12.5 g saturated fat and 0.3 g trans fat), 54.8 g carbohydrate, and 25.2 g sugar, with only 2.2 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 268 mg/100 g. This nutritional profile makes Danish pastry a high-energy, high-fat, high-sugar food with limited fibre. It carries a medium glycaemic index (~59) but a high glycaemic load per standard serving (~25.8 for an 80 g piece), making it clinically significant for blood glucose management. It is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. Danish pastry is commonly consumed as a breakfast item or snack and is widely available in bakeries, cafes, and supermarkets globally, including Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Danish pastry?
Is Danish pastry high in sugar?
What is the GI of Danish pastry?
Is Danish pastry suitable for hypertension?
How much fat is in a Danish pastry?
What is a Danish pastry made of?
Is Danish pastry the same as a croissant?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11110011) at confidence 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source. GI is estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database (laminated pastry / croissant-type), which is an approved GI source; this introduces a minor analogous-estimate caveat but does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the strong macro provenance.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Danish pastry are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record (F11110011) as of April 2026. 2. trans_fat_g of 0.3 g/100 g and saturated_fat_g of ~12.5 g/100 g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for Danish pastry (USDA SR Legacy #18248) as a supporting reference for fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB; this is documented as an adapted value. 3. GI of 59 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for laminated pastry (croissant-type); no exact Danish pastry GI entry was identified in the database as of April 2026. GL per serving = 59 × (54.76 × 0.80) / 100 = 59 × 43.81 / 100 ≈ 25.8. 4. Danish pastry is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. Local names fields are correctly set to null. 5. Halal status is not confirmed as standard Danish pastry recipes use butter and eggs which may or may not be halal-certified depending on the producer; is_halal is set to false as a conservative default.
